Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 7 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the purple indicator changed id=12 back from white to purple. So the top-right indicators toggle the color of id=12 (the top center object). But I need to also change id=13 and id=14.
Current center state:
- id=12: purple [25,26]-[31,37]
- id=13: purple [34,27]-[38,36]  
- id=14: white [39,27]-[43,36]
Left panel reference: white above purple
So the center currently has: purple, purple, white
The goal (left panel) has: white, purple
I need to figure out how to select different objects. Maybe I need to click on the center objects first to "select" them, then use the top-right indicators to paint? Or maybe clicking on the center objects directly changes them?
Let me try clicking on the center objects (id=13 or id=14) to see if that selects them, then clicking an indicator to paint the selected one.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the top-right indicator that's "active" (has a white underline or highlight) determines which color is selected, and clicking on center objects applies that color. Let me look at the top-right area more carefully.
Looking at the initial state, there was a white line (id=8) below the purple indicator at [7,41]-[7,45]. This might indicate that purple is the currently selected color. When I clicked the white indicator, it painted id=12 white. When I clicked purple, it painted id=12 purple.
Wait - maybe clicking on the indicators selects the color, and then clicking on center objects applies it? But earlier when I clicked on the center (id=12 area), nothing happened. Let me try clicking on id=13 or id=14 first.
Or maybe the mechanism is: click on a center object to select it, then click an indicator to paint it. Let me try clicking on id=13 (the middle purple rect) first, then clicking the white indicator.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on center objects to select them, then paint
# First click on id=14 (white rect at [39,27]-[43,36]) to select it
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 31}])
print(f"MOUSE on id=14 (white): board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

if last_action_result.get('board_changed'):
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    for n in after['nodes']:
        bn = [x for x in before['nodes'] if x['id'] == n['id']]
        if bn:
            bn = bn[0]
            if bn['color'] != n['color'] or bn['pixels'] != n['pixels']:
                print(f"  id={n['id']}: color {bn['color']}->{n['color']}, px {bn['pixels']}->{n['pixels']}")

# Now try clicking the purple indicator to see if it paints the selected object
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])
print(f"\nMOUSE on purple indicator: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

if last_action_result.get('board_changed'):
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    for n in after['nodes']:
        bn = [x for x in before['nodes'] if x['id'] == n['id']]
        if bn:
            bn = bn[0]
            if bn['color'] != n['color'] or bn['pixels'] != n['pixels']:
                print(f"  id={n['id']}: color {bn['color']}->{n['color']}, px {bn['pixels']}->{n['pixels']}")

# Check all center objects
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} [{b[0]}]-[{b[2] if len(b)>2 else b[0]}]")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "One-time toggle on center objects", "UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Tick down timer", "MOUSE on top-right indicator": "Paints top center object (id=12) with that color"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_right_indicators": "Color selectors", "center_objects": "Paintable targets"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_strip": "Timer bar", "top_right": "Color palette with active indicator"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Center has 3 objects: id=12 (purple, top), id=13 (purple, mid), id=14 (white, bottom). Top-right indicators paint id=12. Need to figure out how to paint other objects.", "goal_model": "Match center to left panel (white above purple)", "recent_findings": ["Indicators paint id=12 only so far", "Need selection mechanism for other objects"], "open_questions": ["How to select id=13 or id=14 for painting?"], "current_plan": "Click center objects to select, then paint with indicators", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["UP/DOWN only affect timer", "MOUSE on center id=12: no effect"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
